questions.scenario:
A decision record draft says:

Decision: ECS because it is best.
Owner: orders platform team
Notes: use containers

What is the most important missing piece?

options.text: The context and tradeoff: why the checkout API fits ECS/Fargate and what responsibility the team gives up or accepts.
A larger logo for ECS so readers remember the service name.
A promise that rollback will never be needed if the decision is correct.
The plaintext DATABASE_URL so future operators know which database is used.

questions.explanation: A useful record explains reasoning, not just the chosen service. Future teammates need to know the workload shape and tradeoff. Secrets should not be stored in the record.

questions.scenario:
A proposal says:

Decision: Run checkout API on Lambda
Context evidence: long-running HTTP service, existing container image, ALB target health checks, RDS connection pool, steady daytime traffic
Tradeoff: none recorded

What should the reviewer ask first?

options.text: Which event invokes the function and how the team will handle service-like concerns such as connection behavior, timeout, and health evidence.
Which EC2 key pair will be used to SSH into the Lambda execution environment.
Whether the ALB health check path can pull images from ECR.
Whether the decision can skip logs because Lambda hides runtime failures.

questions.explanation: The evidence sounds service-shaped, so a Lambda choice needs clear reasoning. The reviewer should ask how the event, timeout, health, and connection model will work. Without that, the record is probably naming a service rather than explaining a fit.

questions.scenario:
A strong ECS decision record includes:

rollback target: previous task definition and image digest
release evidence: target health, ECS events, CloudWatch logs, smoke test
tradeoff: no host-level control for this API

Why is this useful later?

options.text: It gives operators evidence to verify releases and a known-good runtime recipe to return to if a deploy fails.
It lets the team skip task definitions because the image digest is enough to run production.
It proves EC2 would never be appropriate for any workload in the company.
It stores all secret values in one readable note for easier diagnosis.

questions.explanation: Good records support future operations. They name what was chosen, how to prove it is running, and what rollback means. They do not turn one workload decision into a company-wide rule or a secret document.

questions.scenario:
A compute decision record for the orders platform lists:

checkout API: ECS/Fargate, two tasks behind ALB
receipt worker: Lambda from SQS, idempotency key required
fraud scorer: EC2 because vendor host agent is required
evidence: target health, service events, queue age, function logs, systemd logs

Which qualities make this record useful?

options.text: It chooses compute per workload instead of forcing one answer everywhere.
It names operational evidence for each runtime shape.
It states the exception that justifies EC2 host responsibility.
It hides the reason for each choice so future reviews stay short.

questions.explanation: This record is useful because it keeps decisions scoped and evidence-based. Future engineers can see why each runtime exists and where to look during incidents. Hiding the reasons would make the service names much less helpful.

questions.scenario:
Six months later, a teammate wants to move the receipt worker from Lambda to the main ECS service. Current evidence says:

queue age: normal
duplicate sends: 0 after idempotency fix
function errors: low
checkout latency: stable because email is off the request path

How should the old decision record guide the review?

options.text: Use the record to ask what problem the move solves and what tradeoff changes, rather than changing compute just for uniformity.
Reject every future change because decision records are permanent rules.
Move it immediately because having fewer AWS services is always safer.
Delete the queue metrics because they make the old decision look too specific.

questions.explanation: A decision record is not a cage. It is a memory aid for tradeoffs. If current evidence is healthy, the reviewer should ask what new requirement justifies the move and what operational cost it introduces.
